text: This video describes the technique of radical apical lymph nodes dissection guided by carbon nanoparticles in laparoscopic rectal cancer surgery. This is a sixty-five-year-old man presented with rectal bleeding. The lean is ten centimeters from the anal verge. Preoperative staging shows a three-three rectal cancer.
One day before surgery, the patient received a submucosal injection of one milliliter atavit carbon nanoparticle suspension beside the tumor under colonoscopy via a XXIII-G needle catheter.
Five trocars are used for laparoscopic access. Under laparoscopic view, the tumor appears stained black, and the mesenteric lymph nodes are also visibly marked with black staining.

The surgeon performs radical apical lymph node dissection using a median to lateral approach. The superior hypogastric nerve is clearly visible and protected during the procedure. The colon mesentery is dissected from the median to lateral, revealing the black-stained apical lymph nodes, which are carefully dissected while ensuring nerve protection.
The surgeon continues the dissection of apical lymph nodes towards the cranial side, maintaining careful separation of the stained lymph nodes from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon isolates the inferior mesenteric artery, then performs high ligation and cuts the artery.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the lateral side, exposing the inferior mesentery vein. The vein is then ligated and cut, completing this step of the procedure.
The surgeon harvests six apical lymph nodes, which are visually inspected for metastases. The final pathology indicates stage IIa rectal cancer, with no visible lymph node metastases.
In concluding, carbon nanoparticles is effective as a tracer for enhanced intraoperative identification and dissection of apical lymph nodes in laparoscopic resection for rectal cancer.
